a: A topic is a named channel where related messages live, like a labeled mailbox. Producers write messages into a topic, and consumers read them out. Messages stay in the order they arrived.
fs: A topic is an append-only, ordered log of events that you write to and read from by name.
fj: It is not a queue that deletes a message once someone reads it; Kafka keeps messages around so many readers can each read them.

q: What is a producer in Kafka?
a: A producer is the app that sends (publishes) messages into a topic. It is the writer side of Kafka. Each message it sends is added to the end of the topic's log.
fs: A producer publishes records to a topic and never waits for or knows about who reads them.
fj: A producer does not send messages directly to a consumer; it only writes to the topic, and consumers pull from there independently.

q: What is a consumer in Kafka?
a: A consumer is the app that reads messages from a topic. It is the reader side of Kafka. It pulls messages and processes them, one after another, in order.
fs: A consumer subscribes to a topic and pulls records at its own pace, tracking how far it has read.
fj: Reading a message does not remove it; a consumer just moves its bookmark forward, and the message stays for others.

q: What is a broker in Kafka?
a: A broker is a Kafka server that stores your messages and hands them to consumers. A real Kafka setup runs several brokers together as a cluster, so if one fails the others keep going.
fs: A broker is one server in the cluster that holds data and serves producer writes and consumer reads.
fj: A broker is not your producer or consumer app; it is the Kafka server in the middle that they both connect to.

q: What is an offset in Kafka?
a: An offset is a number that marks a message's position in a topic, like a line number. A consumer remembers the offset it last read, so it can resume from there after a restart instead of starting over.
fs: An offset is a per-partition position; a consumer commits it as a bookmark to resume exactly where it left off.
fj: An offset does not delete or skip messages; it just records how far a consumer has read so it can pick up again.
